title: [ESQL] Add Parquet filter pushdown with bloom filter, statistics, and dictionary row-group skipping
body:
Translates ESQL filter expressions (equality, IN, range comparisons) to
parquet-java FilterPredicate objects. When set on ParquetReadOptions, parquet-java
automatically applies statistics, dictionary, and bloom filter checks to skip
entire row groups that cannot contain matching rows.

Architecture follows the FormatReader-localized pattern: PushFiltersToSource falls back to
FormatReader.filterPushdownSupport() when FilterPushdownRegistry has no entry,
avoiding additions to the registry. FormatReaderRegistry is threaded through
the optimizer context for direct format reader access.

All pushed filters use RECHECK semantics — the original filter stays in
FilterExec for per-row correctness.

Iceberg gets bloom filter support automatically through its internal
ParquetBloomRowGroupFilter when scan filters are set.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling

author: GalLalouche

title: Replace heavyweight Cache with LinkedHashMap in QueryQuantizer
body:
I've been hunting around this area during a recent refactoring and came across this.
 
The `QueryQuantizer` in both `ESNextDiskBBQVectorsReader` and `ES940DiskBBQVectorsReader` used Elasticsearch's `Cache` to store up to 16 quantized query results per centroid ordinal. This cache is created once per search per segment and is accessed single-threaded within the posting visitor loop.

The `Cache` class is designed for shared concurrent access and allocates 256 `CacheSegment` objects, each with a full `ReentrantReadWriteLock` (`NonfairSync`, `ReadLock`, `WriteLock`, `ThreadLocalHoldCounter`). That is ~1,500 objects per `Cache` instance for a 16-entry single-threaded cache. I replaced it with a `LinkedHashMap` in access-order mode using `removeEldestEntry` for LRU eviction. Same 16-entry limit, same eviction callback (reuses the evicted byte array), zero lock overhead.

When profiled with async-profiler (`event=alloc`) on a KnnIndexTester run (cohere-wikipedia-10m, 768d, 1-bit IVF, 100 queries, 8 visit percentages), the `Cache` infrastructure and its locks accounted for 2.4 GB of total sampled allocation (28%). After the change, these allocations are completely eliminated.
author: ChrisHegarty

title: Gate tests on TRANSFORM_CROSS_PROJECT
body:
When running the test in non-snapshot mode the feature flag would be disabled and `ClientTransformIndexer` would run with crossProjectEnabled=false
```
crossProjectEnabled = transformServices.crossProjectModeDecider().crossProjectEnabled()
    && TransformConfig.TRANSFORM_CROSS_PROJECT.isEnabled();
```
which result in test failing with
```
ClientTransformIndexerTests >
testDisablePitWhenCrossProjectSearchIsEnabled FAILED
java.lang.AssertionError: expected null,
but was:<org.elasticsearch.common.bytes.CompositeBytesReference@a72e3d5d>
```

The test only make sense if the feature flag is enabled so now we gate the test on that.

The failure can (could) be reproduced with:
```
 ./gradlew ":x-pack:plugin:transform:test" \--tests "org.elasticsearch
 .xpack.transform.transforms.ClientTransformIndexerTests.testDisablePitWhenCrossProjectSearchIsEnabled" -Dbuild.snapshot=false -Dtests.jvm.argline="-Dbuild.snapshot=false" -Dlicense.key=x-pack/license-tools/src/test/resources/public.key
```
Functionality was introduced here: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/144254

title: PromQL: return empty result when index pattern matches no indices
body:
## Summary

- When the index pattern (always `*` from the Prometheus REST handler) resolves to zero concrete indices, the `PROMQL` ES|QL command now short-circuits to an empty `LocalRelation` in the Analyzer instead of leaving series attributes unresolved.
- Previously this caused a `VerificationException` at analysis time; Prometheus semantics require an empty result (`{"status":"success","data":{"resultType":"matrix","result":[]}}`) when the queried metric does not exist.
- The fix is in `Analyzer.resolvePromql`: when `esRelation.concreteQualifiedIndices().isEmpty()`, return a `LocalRelation([value, step], EMPTY)` — the minimal schema needed for the downstream `EVAL step = TO_LONG(step)` to resolve cleanly.
author: felixbarny

title: [ESQL] Add cloud API rate limiting for external sources
body:
Add three layers of defense against cloud API throttling (503/429)
for S3, GCS, and Azure external source queries:

1. Semaphore-based concurrency limiter (default 50 per scheme per
   node) to cap in-flight cloud API requests
2. Throttle-aware retry policy with higher budget for throttling
   errors (10 retries for 429/503 vs 3 for transient errors)
3. Adaptive backoff that scales retry delays based on observed
   throttling pressure across all requests on the same provider

Only two cluster settings are exposed (dynamic, node-scoped):
- `esql.external.max_concurrent_requests` (default 50, 0=disabled)
- `esql.external.throttle_max_retry_duration` (default 30s, 0=disabled)

Internal defaults (throttle retry count=10, adaptive backoff=always on,
exponential delays with jitter) are hardcoded to de-facto defaults,
 keeping the settings surface minimal.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

title: [ESQL] GCS native async I/O via ReadChannel
body:
Add `readBytes` and `readBytesAsync` overrides to `GcsStorageObject` using GCS `ReadChannel` for direct `ByteBuffer` reads without intermediate `byte[]` allocation. Follows the pattern established by `S3StorageObject`.

**Changes:**
- Override `readBytes(long, ByteBuffer)` with `ReadChannel.read` for zero-copy positional reads
- Override `readBytesAsync(long, long, Executor, ActionListener)` with executor-wrapped ReadChannel reads
- Set `supportsNativeAsync()` to `true`
- Update `GcsStorageProvider` javadoc (remove TODO)
- Add 10 unit tests covering readBytes, readBytesAsync, error handling, and edge cases

**Design notes:**
The async path is executor-based (blocking a worker thread on `ReadChannel`), not truly non-blocking like `HttpClient.sendAsync()` or `S3AsyncClient` — the GCS Java client library does not provide a `CompletableFuture`-based async read API ([googleapis/java-storage#2178](https://github.com/googleapis/java-storage/issues/2178)). The `readBytes` override uses `ReadChannel.read(ByteBuffer)` directly, avoiding the InputStream→byte[]→ByteBuffer copy chain in the default implementation.

This is API-readiness for future parallel column chunk reads. The current Parquet path (`ParquetStorageObjectAdapter`) uses `newStream()` not `readBytes` — end-to-end Parquet improvement requires separate adapter changes.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

title: Report shard snapshot pauses in shutdown status
body:
Today shutdown waits for shard snapshot pauses implicitly by waiting for completion of shard relocation. This works because a shard cannot relocate until its snapshot is paused. Since we intend to remove this dependency in stateless setup, this PR adds explicit wait to shutdown for snapshot pauses. For stateful deployments, this will not add any more waiting time since shard relocations are strictly after it. It provides generally useful information even for stateful to surface the progress of snapshot pausing, something we currently rely solely on the SnapshotShutdownProgressTracker.

Relates ES-14408

title: Fix BinaryDvConfirmedQuery.visit
body:
Today, a single query_string query targeting a wildcard-mapped log index can produce a DisjunctionMaxQuery with thousands of clauses, where each clause is a BinaryDvConfirmedQuery. On a small heap, the max clause limit is low enough that this query should be rejected, but `BinaryDvConfirmedQuery.visit()` does not visit into its approxQuery, which typically contains ~10 ngram boolean clauses. This under-counts the actual clauses, causing the limit to be bypassed.  

```
    "query_string": {
        "fields": {},
        "query": "*1234-8a2a-af71-8353*"
    }
```
author: dnhatn

title: Allow config the docs threshold for auto-partitioning
body:
Today, auto data partitioning uses a hardcoded threshold (250k docs) to decide between shard and doc partitioning. Below that threshold, shard partitioning is used because the overhead of doc partitioning outweighs the concurrency benefits for small shards.

However, this threshold is not configurable, making it difficult to exercise the doc-partitioning path in tests. This change exposes the threshold as a cluster setting (`esql.docs_threshold_auto_partitioning`) and query pragma, allowing us to trigger this path more often in tests.

title: Temporarily allow warning header pattern to match with or without semantic version
body:
The warning header agent pattern is selected at startup based on
whether the current build has a semantic version. When a build
extension changes the version from a non-semantic value (e.g. a hash)
to a real semantic version, the pattern must still match warning
headers produced by older nodes using the previous format. Make the
semantic version portion optional and the hash portion always required.

This is a temporary change needed during the transition period where
mixed clusters may contain nodes using both formats.
author: rjernst

title: ESQL: Add LZ4, Snappy, and Brotli decompression codecs
body:
Add three new file-level decompression codecs enabling compound
extensions like .csv.lz4, .ndjson.snappy, and .csv.br for external
data sources.

Introduce a shared esql-datasource-compression-libs plugin that
bundles aircompressor and zstd-jni once, replacing duplicate copies
previously bundled independently by ORC and the Zstd codec. Format
plugins (Parquet, ORC, Iceberg) and codec plugins (Snappy, Zstd)
extend compression-libs via extendedPlugins to share the same
loaded copies through parent-first classloader delegation.

LZ4 uses lz4-java already on the server classpath via libs/lz4.
Brotli uses org.brotli:dec (MIT, ~60KB, pure Java decoder).
Snappy enables CRC-32C verification by default for data integrity.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

title: ESQL: Add ORC predicate pushdown via SearchArgument
body:
Enables the ORC format reader to leverage file, stripe, and row-group level column statistics for predicate pushdown. The ORC library's SearchArgument API is used to skip stripes and row groups whose statistics prove they cannot contain matching rows.

Uses RECHECK semantics — the SearchArgument performs block-level skipping only, not row-level filtering. The ESQL FilterExec always remains in the plan for row-level correctness.

Expression conversion follows a two-pass approach: validate convertibility first, then build the SearchArgument. Supports comparison operators, IN, IS NULL/NOT NULL, Range, and boolean connectives (AND/OR/NOT). Bloom filters are evaluated automatically by the ORC library for equality and IN predicates.

Adds format-aware pushdown dispatch to the FilterPushdownRegistry so file-based formats (which share source type "file") can register pushdown support independently.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

title: Docs: clarify geo_shape spatial relations (#70662)
body:
## Summary

title: Split native and Panama OSQ vector scorer implementations
body:
The OSQ vector scorer methods have grown cluttered with nested `if/else` branches testing `NATIVE_SUPPORTED`, `SUPPORTS_HEAP_SEGMENTS`, and `VECTOR_BITSIZE` , all static final constants. I am concerned about maintenance and JIT inlining, since the large bytecode size of these methods could prevent the JVM from inlining them on the hot path. So I split the native (FFI) and Panama (Vector API) implementations into separate class hierarchies. 

A `NativeMemorySegmentScorer` abstract base provides template methods for quantize, bulk scoring, and corrections. Three small final subclasses (one per quant type) supply only the native dot-product function pointers and bit scales. The existing `MS*` scorers are now Panama-only, with all native branches removed. The right implementation is selected once at class-load time via a static factory map, keyed on query/index bit combination. 

I also moved the `dataLength >= 16` SIMD minimum check from every method call to construction time, so the native hot-path methods have no unnecessary runtime guards. A nice consequence of this is that the filtered KNN path (`scoreBulkOffsets`) now uses fully native scoring, both quantize and bulk corrections end-to-end. Previously it used native quantize but fell back to scalar corrections. I also replaced `readInt()` loops with bulk `readInts()` in the base `ESNextOSQVectorsScorer`.

title: Fix geo_centroid over geo_shape merging multiple shards
body:
As described in https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/144504 we are seeing different results between `geo_centroid` in QueryDSL and ES|QL's `ST_CENTROID_AGG` when the data is `geo_shape` (or cartesian shapes). The root cause turned out to be that `geo_centriod` was using point-only logic for merging intermediate aggregation results between shards, which means it was considering doc-count only for the weighted merge, and missing:
* Area weights (large polygons count more than small ones, and long lines count more than short ones)
* Dimensional type (shards with only points should not count at all if other shards contain lines or polygons)

This PR contains appropriate unit tests, but manual testing on the OSM dataset we used in the benchmarking leads to:

| test | ESQL doc count | ESQL centroid | Query DSL doc count| QueryDSL centroid |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| original | 3818897 | POINT (0.7135620032440881 54.048676344286505) | 3818897 | {lat: 52.91002138728532, lon: 4.350566187678227} |
| after area-weight fix | 3818897 | POINT (0.7135620032440897 54.04867634428662) | 3818897 | {lat: 51.63239198052063, lon: 8.3761283280999} |
| after dimensional-type fix | 3818897 | POINT (0.7135620032440892 54.04867634428658) | 2017758 | {lat: 54.048676344287394, lon: 0.7135620032440999 } |

Note: the small deviations on the 15th and 16th decimal point of the ES|QL results actually occur when simply re-running the same query over and over and are likely a result of floating point errors related to the order in which results are added to the [Kahan summation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kahan_summation_algorithm) and are not related to the fixes.

Fixes #144504

title: Refactor native SIMD mappers to return pointers directly
body:
This commit refactors the native vector scoring templates so that mapper functions return a direct `const T*` pointer to the target vector, instead of an `int64_t` byte offset that callers then multiply and add to a base pointer. This simplifies every bulk scoring template (`call_i8_bulk`, `call_f32_bulk`, `cosi8_inner_bulk`, `dot7u_inner_bulk`, `sqr7u_inner_bulk`, `doti4_bulk_impl`, `dotdXq4_inner_bulk`) by collapsing the three-step resolve-offset-then-compute-pointer pattern into a single mapper call.

Specifically:
- **`vec_common.h`**: `identity_mapper` and `array_mapper` are now templates returning `const T*`. Removed `safe_mapper_offset` and `init_offsets`; replaced with `init_pointers` which combines pointer resolution and bounds checking in one step.
- **aarch64 and amd64 `.cpp` files**: Updated all template signatures, mapper call sites, and `init_offsets` /
    `safe_mapper_offset` usages to use the new pointer-returning mappers and `init_pointers`.

Motivation

The previous design required each call site to perform `base + mapper(i, offsets) * pitch` to obtain a vector pointer. With mappers returning pointers directly, call sites are simpler and the mapper signature becomes extensible; new mappers (e.g. for non-contiguous memory layouts) can use completely different addressing strategies while conforming to the same function pointer type. We have such things in the works!
author: ChrisHegarty

title: Ensure cancellation reason visible when task is canceled (#142079)
body:
Although, the cancellation reason is set in a synchronized block the access to the `reason` variable is not synchronized so there operation is not atomic. 

By removing an additional field (`isCancelled`) and replacing its usage by comparing the `reason` to `null` we can achieve the same behavior without a risk of `reason` field being null when the task was canceled. 

To efficiently compare and set the `reason`, `java.lang.invoke.VarHandle` was used.

IMHO this is a genuine bug caught by a test that is currently muted. This PR fixes #142079 issue.

title: Write prefix partition for tsid in tsdb codec
body:
Follow-up to #143955, which introduced a single-byte metric prefix in the tsid layout.

This PR writes prefix partition metadata for the `_tsid` field. The `_tsid` field is grouped by its first 2 bytes - the metric prefix byte (byte-0) plus one random byte (byte-1) - yielding up to 256 partitions per metric. The partition records the starting document for each prefix group, allowing the query engine to slice data so that each slice contains only time-series sharing the same prefix.

This enables ESQL to partition work across slices without splitting any individual time-series - a requirement for aggregations like rate. This should reduce memory usage and improve performance compared to time-interval partitioning, which requires multiple queries over fragmented data. 

The compute engine is not wired up yet, so no improvements are expected yet, but this change may cause a small regression in indexing throughput and storage overhead, which is expected to be trivial.

Relates #143955

title: [ESQL] Fix first/last functions when used with timeseries
body:
A recent alert uncovered crashes in TS queries with errors like:

```text
Plan [Aggregate[[pack_attributes.session.id_$1{r}#5067],[LAST(DEFAULTTIMESERIESAGGREGATEFUNCTION_$1{r}#5065,true[BOOLEAN],PT0S[TIME_DURATION],@timestamp{f}#4994) AS final_session_cost#4801]]] optimized incorrectly due to missing references [@timestamp{f}#4994]
```

Root cause: 

This happens when  LAST(field, sortBy) or FIRST(field, sortBy) is used with with TS queries. Time series aggregates are performed in two stages: first by _tsid and bucket (if provided) and then by whatever aggregate function supply. The first phase leaves bare sortBy (e.g. `@timestamp`), so the second phase ends up referencing an attribute not produced by the first phase.

Fix: 

Wrap the sortBy argument of LAST/FIRST with MAX/MIN. Both are `TimeSeriesAggregateFunction` subclasses, and existing translation pipeline handles them naturally during the split, with no extra handling.

Issue: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/144411

title: [ESQL] ESQL: Route external source I/O through esql_worker pool
body:
External source operators (`EXTERNAL/FROM external_source`) previously dispatched I/O tasks to the `SEARCH` thread pool, competing with regular ES search operations. This change routes external source I/O through `esql_worker` instead, and introduces `externalSourceExecutor()` as the single extension point for a future dedicated I/O pool.

In general ES tends to be conservative around introducing new thread pools due to the large surface area around monitoring, support, and configuration which can lead to oversubscription. While a dedicated I/O pool separate from compute makes long-term sense, `esql_worker` is the right intermediate step: the immediate starvation problem is solved, `EXTERNAL/FROM external_source` is a new command so no existing workloads are disrupted, and the `externalSourceExecutor()` method means switching to a dedicated pool later is a one-method change.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

title: Add metrics prefix to Prometheus remote write routing paths
body:
## Background

The Prometheus remote write REST action registered these routes:

- `POST /_prometheus/api/v1/write` → `metrics-generic.prometheus-default`
- `POST /_prometheus/{dataset}/api/v1/write` → `metrics-{dataset}.prometheus-default`
- `POST /_prometheus/{dataset}/{namespace}/api/v1/write` → `metrics-{dataset}.prometheus-{namespace}`

The `{dataset}` wildcard sitting directly at position 2 (after `/_prometheus/`) creates a hard structural conflict with the long-term design of `/_prometheus/{index}/api/v1/...` for future query endpoints (e.g. `query_range`, `series`). Elasticsearch's `PathTrie` enforces that all routes sharing the same wildcard position use the same parameter name — registering both `/_prometheus/{dataset}/...` and `/_prometheus/{index}/...` would throw `IllegalArgumentException: Trying to use conflicting wildcard names for same path: dataset and index` at startup.

## Change

Inserting a fixed `metrics` literal at position 2 resolves the conflict. The new routing paths are:

- `POST /_prometheus/api/v1/write` → `metrics-generic.prometheus-default` (unchanged)
- `POST /_prometheus/metrics/{dataset}/api/v1/write` → `metrics-{dataset}.prometheus-default`
- `POST /_prometheus/metrics/{dataset}/{namespace}/api/v1/write` → `metrics-{dataset}.prometheus-{namespace}`

The `metrics` prefix is also self-documenting — it mirrors the `metrics` data stream type that all Prometheus remote write data lands in, and it constrains routing correctly (Prometheus metrics can only target the `metrics` data stream type).

Only the REST routing is affected. The transport action, all business logic, and the no-routing default path are unchanged.
author: felixbarny

title: Improve quantile interpolation stability for exponential histograms
body:
Fixes #144569.

The exponential histogram quantile algorithm interpolates linearly between percentiles falling on fractional ranks.
E.g. if a percentile corresponds to the value at rank `42.3`, we linearly interpolate between the values at rank `42` and `43`.

This interpolation was implemented in a way, that it could break percentile monotonicity (higher percentile => higher or equal result value) due to floating point errors.

This PR fixes this problem by changing the code how the interpolation is performed and it adds an assertion for monotonicity to `QuantileAccuracyTests` which easily reproduced the error on almost every randomized run.

title: Add multi-tenant KNN benchmark support to KnnIndexTester
body:
Adds synthetic multi-tenant data generation and per-tenant filtered KNN search benchmarking to `KnnIndexTester` tool. This enables measuring recall and performance degradation for vector search in multi-tenant scenarios without requiring pre-built datasets.

### Motivations

Tenants share a single index but queries are filtered to a specific tenant. We need tooling to benchmark how recall and latency behaves as the number of tenants scales, and how document distribution across tenants (uniform vs. skewed) affects search quality.

### Changes

- **`MultiTenantDataGenerator`** — New class that generates synthetic random vectors and assigns documents to tenants. Supports two distributions:
  - `uniform` — equal documents per tenant
  - `zipf` — power-law skewed (some tenants get many more documents)

- **`TestConfiguration`** — Four new config fields: `use_data_generator`, `num_tenants`, `tenant_distribution`, `generator_seed`

- **`KnnIndexer`** — New `createGeneratedIndex()` method that write documents sorted by `tenant_id` (using `SortedDocValuesField` + `StringField`), with `IndexWriterConfig.setIndexSort` for contiguous tenant document placement

- **`KnnSearcher`** — New `runMultiTenantSearch()` method that runs filtered KNN queries per tenant using `TermQuery`, computes brute-force exact NN with the same filter for ground truth, and reports per-tenant recall

- **`KnnIndexTester`** — Wired generator mode into `runTestConfiguration()`, added `runMultiTenantSearches()` with warm-up support, `perTenantRecall` field in `Results`, per-tenant recall summary in formatted output

### Example Configuration

```json
{
  "use_data_generator": true,
  "num_docs": 100000,
  "dimensions": 128,
  "num_tenants": 50,
  "tenant_distribution": "uniform",
  "generator_seed": 42,
  "index_type": "hnsw",
  "hnsw_m": 16,
  "hnsw_ef_construction": 100,
  "vector_space": "COSINE",
  "vector_encoding": "FLOAT32",
  "num_queries": 500,
  "reindex": true,
  "force_merge": true,
  "search_params": [{"num_candidates": 100, "k": 10}]
}
```

### Benchmark Results

All benchmarks run with HNSW (m=16, efConstruction=100), 100k docs, 128 dimensions, 500 queries, COSINE similarity, `num_candidates=100`, `k=10`.

#### 50 tenants, uniform distribution

Index Build

| index_name                                         | index_type | num_docs | total_index_time(ms) | force_merge_time(ms) | num_segments |
|----------------------------------------------------|-----------|---------:|---------------------:|---------------------:|-------------:|
| target/knn_index/generated-50-tenants-16-100.index | hnsw      | 100000   | 33417                | 2                    | 1            |

Query Performance

| index_name                                         | index_type | latency(ms) | QPS  | recall | visited | num_candidates |
|----------------------------------------------------|-----------|------------:|-----:|------:|--------:|---------------:|
| target/knn_index/generated-50-tenants-16-100.index | hnsw      | 0.37        | 2732 | 0.42  | 677.85  | 100            |

Per-tenant Recall

| tenants | min   | max   | avg   |
|--------:|------:|------:|------:|
| 50      | 0.2000| 0.6364| 0.4235 |

#### 50 tenant, Zipf distribution

| index_name                                          | index_type | latency (ms) | QPS  | recall | visited | num_candidates |
|-----------------------------------------------------|------------|--------------|------|--------|---------|----------------|
| target/knn_index/generated-50-tenants-16-100.index  | hnsw       | 0.26         | 3875 | 0.71   | 879.07  | 100            |

**Per-tenant recall**

| tenants | min   | max   | avg   |
|--------:|------:|------:|------:|
| 50      | 0.3000| 0.9667| 0.7121 |

#### 200 tenants, uniform distribution
| index_name                                          | index_type | latency(ms) | QPS  | recall | visited | num_candidates |
|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------|-------------|------|--------|--------|----------------|
| target/knn_index/generated-200-tenants-16-100.index | hnsw      | 0.13        | 7936 | 0.91   | 500.00 | 100            |

Per-tenant recall:

| tenants | min   | max   | avg   |
|--------|-------|-------|-------|
| 177    | 0.8000 | 1.0000 | 0.9094 |

### Key Observations

| Scenario | Avg Recall | Min Recall | Max Recall | QPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50 tenants, uniform (2000 docs/tenant) | 0.42 | 0.20 | 0.64 | 2,732 |
| 50 tenants, zipf (skewed) | 0.71 | 0.30 | 0.97 | 3,876 |
| 200 tenants, uniform (500 docs/tenant) | 0.91 | 0.80 | 1.00 | 7,937 |

- **Recall degrades with larger per-tenant doc counts**: With 2000 docs/tenant (50 tenants uniform), recall drops to ~0.42 as the HNSW graph traversal visits neighbors from other tenants that get filtered out.
- **Zipf improves recall for small tenants**: Skewed distributions mean most tenants have fewer documents, yielding higher recall for the majority. Larger tenants see lower recall.
- **Smaller tenants achieve near-perfect recall**: With 500 docs/tenant (200 tenants), `num_candidates=100` is sufficient to find all 10 nearest neighbors within a tenant, yielding 0.91 average recall.
- **QPS increases with tenant count**: Fewer documents per tenant means less work per filtered query.

These results confirm the expected recall vs. tenant-density tradeoff and establish a baseline for evaluating future improvements such as index-sort-aware HNSW graph construction.

commits.message:
Add synthetic multi-tenant data for KNN tests

Introduce a synthetic multi-tenant data generator for KNN
benchmarking with configurable tenant counts and document
distributions, including uniform and Zipf. Update indexing and
search logic to measure and report per-tenant recall. Add test
configuration parameters for data generation to improve
flexibility for multi-tenant search scenarios and benchmarking
accuracy.

Refactors tenant to partition-based benchmarking

Unifies synthetic data generation and search evaluation around a
partition-based abstraction instead of tenants, simplifying
configuration and code paths. Introduces a structured dataset config
supporting both GCP and generated datasets. Updates search recall
reporting to per-partition and adjusts result summaries accordingly.
Reduces legacy config options and clarifies dataset selection.

This enables more flexible benchmarking scenarios and streamlines
future extensibility.

Refactors idx/srch for unify partition/file logic

Unifies partitioned and file-based indexing and search flows by
introducing shared setup and search abstractions. Simplifies
branching, reduces code duplication, and improves maintainability.
Enables easier extension for new data generation and query strategies
while retaining support for recall and metrics reporting per partition
or globally.

Introduce DataGenerator interface for KNN test data

Extracts data provisioning logic into a DataGenerator interface with
FileDataGenerator and PartitionDataGenerator implementations, replacing
conditional branching based on dataset type throughout the orchestration
code.

Each DatasetConfig variant now creates its own DataGenerator via a
factory method, encapsulating indexing and search setup logic closer
to where the dataset-specific knowledge lives.

Also adopts NamedXContent-style parsing for dataset configuration,
where the object key identifies the dataset type (e.g., "gcp" or
"partition_generated"), removing the inner "type" field. Adds
comprehensive test coverage for dataset config parsing and roundtrip
serialization.

Add file dataset type for KNN vector testing

Adds a "file" dataset variant to DatasetConfig, allowing users to
specify local doc and query vector file paths directly in the test
configuration instead of requiring a GCP bucket download.

Caches brute-force exact partitioned nearest neighbor results to disk
to avoid expensive recomputation across runs.

Conditionally hides partition recall columns in result output when
no partition data is present, keeping tables cleaner for non-partitioned
datasets.

Refactor vector reader and data classes into separate files and package

Extracts IndexVectorReader and its implementations (FileVectorReader,
MultiFileVectorReader, PartitionGeneratingVectorReader, VectorReader)
from KnnIndexer into a dedicated top-level interface file, improving
code organization and separation of concerns.

Moves data-related classes (DataGenerator, DatasetConfig,
FileDataGenerator, PartitionDataGenerator) into a new `data` subpackage
to better reflect their role and reduce coupling with the indexer.

Widens visibility of key classes, records, and fields to public to
support usage from the new package structure.

Adds ToXContentFragment to DatasetConfig so each variant serializes
itself, replacing the instanceof-based serialization in TestConfiguration.

Replaces deprecated SortedDocValuesField + StringField combination with
SortedDocValuesField.indexedField for partition documents.

Fixes minor issues: corrects a log message typo ("docsPath" → "queryPath"),
converts string concatenation to parameterized logging, and adds unit
tests for PartitionDataGenerator distributions.

Clean up KNN vector test infrastructure

Encapsulates KnnSearcher fields behind accessor methods instead of
exposing public fields directly.

Makes IndexVectorReader extend Closeable to simplify resource
management and removes redundant instanceof checks.

Converts partition distribution from raw strings to a type-safe enum,
catching invalid values at parse time rather than lazily at runtime.

Removes string shorthand parsing for GCP dataset config, keeping only
the explicit object form for consistency.

Simplifies indexing counter logic using getAndIncrement instead of
compareAndSet loop, and uses IOUtils/CollectionUtils for cleaner
collection and resource handling.

Simplify stats computation using summaryStatistics

Replaces manual min/max/sum tracking with Java's DoubleSummaryStatistics
stream API for computing partition recall statistics.

Also converts if-else chains to switch expressions for dataset config
handling, improving readability.

Resolve merge conflicts in KNN benchmark code

Cleans up unresolved merge conflict markers between knn-multi-tenant-benchmark
and main branches in KnnIndexer, KnnSearcher, and FileDataGenerator.

Merges both branches' features: retains the documentFactory abstraction from
the multi-tenant branch while preserving vector normalization support from main.
Adds missing l2normalize call for float query vectors in FileDataGenerator.

title: Consolidate persistent task toggleable lifecycle logic
body:
Consolidates the duplicated enable/disable persistent task lifecycle logic shared by `HealthNodeTaskExecutor` and `GeoIpDownloaderTaskExecutor` into a single `PersistentTaskLifecycleManager` service.

Relates to ES-14293

More context on why we are switching to a cluster state listener based lifecycle here: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/143915#discussion_r2916441224.

This PR follows the naive attempt at implementing the `GeoIpDownloaderTaskExecutor` cluster state listener logic here: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/144374#issuecomment-4071774706.

It precedes removing the `automaticReassignmentOnShutdown` flag -> which will be done in #144374
author: inespot

reviews.body: I left a comment.

I like that we're consolidating this logic, it's clearly too trappy to expect each task to do the work itself. However I'm uneasy about this design, particularly wondering if it's really the job of the `PersistentTasksExecutor` to manage the lifecycle of the task entry in the cluster state. All persistent tasks need to manage these lifecycles somehow, but today they basically all do so using some component that works independently of the `PersistentTasksExecutor`, apart from these cluster-wide things like the health task. This feels to me like something we could pull apart.

Would it instead make sense to extract a separate component which watches the setting and creates/removes the task entry as needed? Or rather, have one such component for cluster-level tasks and another for project-level ones.

I am in two minds about whether to preserve the current behaviour faithfully or to fix some shortcomings. For instance:

- on failure, if we're still the elected master, I think we should retry immediately rather than waiting for the next cluster state update
- I don't think there should be a timeout involved: if the request times out then we will eventually retry, from the back of the queue, which seems worse than waiting more patiently
- we shouldn't submit multiple start/stop requests at once for each task. I suspect we should try to avoid having both a start and a stop request in flight at once.

I think we might also want to come back to the `GeoIpDownloader` task in a followup - the relationship with the existence of the index is a little subtle (see inline comment)

title: Replaced IntegratedCount with SeparateCount for binary doc value fields
body:
With `multi_value = no`, it would be nice to automatically figure out whether we used a `MultiValuedBinaryDocValuesField` or a `BinaryDocValuesField`. Doing so eliminates the need to pass `multiValue` all over the place and perform if-checks. The best place to do that would be [here](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/blob/5c1c9209b2910b2e0206ba0d732ee1917af4d513/server/src/main/java/org/elasticsearch/index/fielddata/MultiValuedSortedBinaryDocValues.java#L45); ie. if there is no `.count` field, we assume that only a single-value was written. Unfortunately, this check is already taken by the `IntegratedCount` format.

That being said, `IntegratedCount` and `SeparateCount` should, in theory, be interchangeable. They're both used for the same thing and the storage footprint of both is equal, with some exceptions where `SeparateCount` is actually more efficient. As a result, we can remove `IntegratedCount` entirely. 

Note, for BWC purposes, `IntegratedCount` remains, gated behind an index version check.

Most of the code changes are attributed to two new tests that I've added: `MultiValuedSortedBinaryDocValuesTests` and `MultiValuedBinaryDocValuesFieldTests`.

This addresses https://github.com/elastic/logs-program/issues/55.

author: lukewhiting

commits.message:
Update timeout on yaml rest tests because they often exceed 1h (#142715)

What is happening now is that whichever test happens to be running when
the timeout threshold is crossed will get muted by the CI automation.
Once sufficient tests are muted, the timeout is no longer crossed. This
means arbitrary tests are muted, not the ones that actually take the
longest.

Extending the timeout is an interim measure and should be followed up by
one or both of these options: * Investigate the slowest tests and decide
if they are needed or could be simplified, and try to reduce the total
time to within reason * Split the tests into groups that are run on
separate test runs, probably in parallel. This costs more in
infrastructure, but with less risk of reduce coverage

Fixes #141113 Fixes #141856

(cherry picked from commit 97e43968417bff1bb4251d2835326ecfb7f79854)

title: [ESQL] Propagate per-split statistics through FileSplit
body:
Enable metadata-only aggregates (COUNT(*), MIN, MAX) for multi-split external
source queries. When row-group parallelism creates multiple splits per file,
each split now carries per-row-group statistics extracted from Parquet metadata.
The optimizer rules merge these per-split statistics instead of falling back to
a full scan, enabling zero-I/O aggregate resolution across splits.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

reviews.body: Will LG after some small changes, serialisation might need to be checked.

commits.message:
ESQL: Propagate per-split statistics through FileSplit

Enable metadata-only aggregates (COUNT(*), MIN, MAX) even when
row-group parallelism creates multiple splits per file. Each split
now carries per-row-group statistics extracted during Parquet split
discovery. The optimizer rules merge these per-split statistics
(sum row counts, min-of-mins, max-of-maxes) instead of bailing
out when splits > 1.

Key changes:
- Add SplitRange record to RangeAwareFormatReader SPI
- Add statistics field to FileSplit with boolean-flag serialization
- Add mergeStatistics() to SourceStatisticsSerializer
- ParquetFormatReader extracts per-row-group stats in discoverSplitRanges
- Both PushStatsToExternalSource and PushAggregatesToExternalSource
  merge per-split stats for multi-split queries

title: PromQL: validate that metric fields are not dimensions in postAnalysisVerification
body:
Using a dimension field (e.g. a keyword label like `host` or `pod`) as a PromQL metric name previously caused a 5xx server error deep in execution (`EsqlIllegalArgumentException.illegalDataType`). This triggers alerts because 5xx responses indicate server errors, not user errors.

- Added a check in `PromqlCommand#postAnalysisVerification` that rejects series fields tagged as TSDB dimension fields with a clear user-facing error
- Absent metrics (fields that don't exist and are nullified to `DataType.NULL` by `ResolveUnmapped`) are excluded from the check, preserving existing Prometheus-style empty-result semantics
- Also allows `aggregate_metric_double` fields as valid metric types, fixing broken TSDB tests that used that type
- Fixed the generative PromQL tests to not generate queries using keyword/string fields as metric selectors, as those are always dimension fields in TSDB
- Added two tests: `testRateOnNonNumericField` and `testAggregationOnNonNumericField`

Note: this validation is intentionally limited to dimension fields. Non-dimension non-numeric fields used with arithmetic functions can still cause a 5xx. The proper fix is tracked in #145384.
author: felixbarny

commits.message:
PromQL: validate that metric fields are numeric in postAnalysisVerification

Using a non-numeric field (e.g. a keyword dimension/label) as a PromQL metric
name previously caused a 5xx server error deep in execution. This change catches
the type mismatch early in `PromqlCommand#postAnalysisVerification`, producing a
clear user-facing 4xx-style error instead.

Absent metrics (nullified to DataType.NULL by ResolveUnmapped) are excluded from
the check so that Prometheus-style empty-result semantics are preserved.

PromQL: also allow aggregate_metric_double as a valid metric type

aggregate_metric_double is not considered numeric by DataType.isNumeric()
but is a valid TSDB field type for PromQL metrics.

PromQL: reject dimension fields used as metrics instead of all non-numeric types

The previous check blocked legitimate use cases like count_over_time(boolean[10m])
and present_over_time(date_nanos[10m]). Dimension fields (keyword labels, IPs, etc.)
are the clear-cut invalid case — they can never be metric values.

PromQL generative tests: don't use string/keyword fields as metric selectors

Keyword fields in TSDB are typically dimension fields, which cannot be used
as metric selectors. Use numeric or date fields instead for the default case.

title: Add Linux-based cross-compilation pipeline for libvec native binaries
body:
This PR adds a Docker-based cross-compilation pipeline for building `libvec` for all three platforms from a single Linux/amd64 container, replacing the previous Mac-dependent workflow. It builds on top of #145066 (merged), which introduced the tinystd headers and builtin replacements.

The three output binaries are built using:

| Platform | File | Compiler |
|---|---|---|
| `darwin-aarch64` | `libvec.dylib` | `clang++-18` → Mach-O via lld |
| `linux-aarch64` | `libvec.so` | `aarch64-linux-gnu-g++-14` |
| `linux-x64` | `libvec.so` | `g++-14` (native) |

## Docker image

```
debian:trixie-slim
       │
       ▼
es-native-cross-toolchain:N     Dockerfile.cross-toolchain
  clang-18 + lld-18             (built from any machine, pushed when
  gcc-14 + g++-14                toolchain versions change)
  g++-14-aarch64-linux-gnu
  make
       │
       ▼ (docker run --platform linux/amd64 -v $(pwd):/workspace)
  make all → libvec.dylib + libvec.so × 2
```

The Docker image is a single public layer — **no macOS SDK** is included. This avoids any licensing concerns around redistributing Apple's proprietary SDK in a Docker image.

### How does Darwin cross-compilation work without the SDK?

The Makefile uses `-nostdinc` to block host (glibc) headers from leaking into the Darwin build, then adds the tinystd headers (from #145066) and clang's freestanding builtins (`arm_neon.h`, `stdint.h`, etc.) via explicit `-isystem` paths:

```makefile
CLANG = clang++-18 --target=arm64-apple-macos14 -nostdinc \
          -isystem src/vec/headers/darwin/tinystd \
          -isystem $(CLANG_RESOURCE)/include
```

At link time, `-nostdlib -Wl,-undefined,dynamic_lookup` avoids needing SDK stub libraries. The only remaining runtime symbols (`_bzero`, `dyld_stub_binder`) are resolved by macOS's dyld at load time.

Linux builds are unaffected — they use the real libstdc++ from GCC as before.

### Additional cleanup in this PR

- Added `-DNDEBUG` and `-Winline` to CXXFLAGS
- Removed redundant `#pragma` target push/pop from `_2.cpp` files (the Makefile compiles them with the correct `-march`)
- Removed standalone Gradle build (`gradlew`/`build.gradle`) — replaced by `make local`/`make install`
- Removed obsolete Dockerfiles (`Dockerfile.aarch64`, `Dockerfile.amd64`)

---

## ISA tiers

Source files are split by naming convention. `*_2.cpp` files implement
tier-2 ISA paths and are compiled with a higher `-march`; all other files
use the baseline.

| Target | Tier 1 (`*_1.cpp`, `caps.cpp`, …) | Tier 2 (`*_2.cpp`) |
|---|---|---|
| `darwin-aarch64` | `armv8.2-a+dotprod` | `armv8.2-a+sve` |
| `linux-aarch64` | `armv8.2-a+dotprod` | `armv8.2-a+sve` |
| `linux-x64` | `core-avx2` | `icelake-client` |

The Makefile compiles each group to `.o` files separately, then links them into the shared library.

---

## Workflows

### Publish a new release

Requires `ARTIFACTORY_API_KEY` to be set. Bump `VERSION` in
`publish_vec_binaries.sh` first.

```bash
./publish_vec_binaries.sh
```

### Local test build (no upload)

Produces `vec-<VERSION>-local.zip` in the working directory.

```bash
./publish_vec_binaries.sh --local
```

### Local build with upload

Build using the local Docker image, but upload to Artifactory.
Useful when you don't have access to the registry image but want to publish.

```bash
./publish_vec_binaries.sh --local --force-upload
```

### Update compiler versions

Bump `VERSION` in `build_cross_toolchain_image.sh`, update package
versions in `Dockerfile.cross-toolchain`, then:

```bash
./build_cross_toolchain_image.sh          # build and push
./build_cross_toolchain_image.sh --local  # build locally only
```

After pushing, update `TOOLCHAIN_IMAGE` in `publish_vec_binaries.sh`.

---

## Verification

Tested on all three platforms:
- **darwin-aarch64** (Mac): Docker + tinystd — all JDKVectorLibrary tests pass
- **linux-aarch64** (ARM EC2 c8gd.xlarge): native make + g++-14 — all tests pass, binary byte-identical to previous artifactory release
- **linux-x64** (Intel EC2 c8i.2xlarge): native make + g++-14 — all tests pass

## Local development

The standalone Gradle build (`gradlew`/`build.gradle`) has been removed. Use the Makefile instead:

```bash
# Build the library for the current platform (macOS, Linux x64, or Linux ARM)
make local

# Build and copy to the Gradle test directory
make install

# Run tests with the locally built library
LOCAL_VEC_BINARY_OS=darwin ./gradlew :libs:native:test --tests "..."   # macOS
LOCAL_VEC_BINARY_OS=linux  ./gradlew :libs:native:test --tests "..."   # Linux
```

title: [9.2] Update timeout on yaml rest tests because they often exceed 1h (#142715)
body:
# Backport

This will backport the following commits from `main` to `9.2`:
 - [Update timeout on yaml rest tests because they often exceed 1h (#142715)](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/142715)

<!--- Backport version: 11.0.1 -->

### Questions ?
Please refer to the [Backport tool documentation](https://github.com/sorenlouv/backport)
author: lukewhiting

commits.message:
Update timeout on yaml rest tests because they often exceed 1h (#142715)

What is happening now is that whichever test happens to be running when
the timeout threshold is crossed will get muted by the CI automation.
Once sufficient tests are muted, the timeout is no longer crossed. This
means arbitrary tests are muted, not the ones that actually take the
longest.

Extending the timeout is an interim measure and should be followed up by
one or both of these options: * Investigate the slowest tests and decide
if they are needed or could be simplified, and try to reduce the total
time to within reason * Split the tests into groups that are run on
separate test runs, probably in parallel. This costs more in
infrastructure, but with less risk of reduce coverage

Fixes #141113 Fixes #141856

(cherry picked from commit 97e43968417bff1bb4251d2835326ecfb7f79854)

title: [ESQL] Enable cross-node text file splitting
body:
Enable byte-range splitting for text formats (CSV, NDJSON, etc.)
by changing the default target split size from disabled (-1) to
64 MB — 2x the max compression block target to keep memory
pressure low. Large files now produce multiple FileSplits
distributed across compute nodes, complementing the existing
intra-node ParallelParsingCoordinator. Byte-range splits carry
FIRST/LAST split markers for correct header and boundary handling.

Also bumps schema inference sample size from 100 to 20,000 rows
for both CSV and NDJSON, reducing type mis-inference risk on files
where data patterns change after the first 100 rows.

New WITH clause options:
- target_split_size: override split size (e.g. "64mb")
- schema_sample_size: override CSV inference sample (e.g. 500)

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

reviews.body: LG, but there might be some inconsistency CSV vs NDJSON.

title: [ESQL] Implement RangeAwareFormatReader for ORC
body:
Enable stripe-level split parallelism for ORC files by implementing
RangeAwareFormatReader. `discoverSplitRanges()` returns per-stripe byte
ranges with statistics (row count, size, per-column min/max/null count),
and `readRange()` restricts reading to stripes within a given byte range
via ORC's `Reader.Options.range()`. Refactors shared read configuration
into reusable helpers for consistency between `read()` and `readRange()`.

Builds on top of #145579 which introduced the same for Parquet and the
`SplitRange` record with per-split statistics support.

Developed with AI-assisted tooling
author: costin

reviews.body: LG, except serialisation and some small remarks.

commits.message:
ESQL: Propagate per-split statistics through FileSplit

Enable metadata-only aggregates (COUNT(*), MIN, MAX) even when
row-group parallelism creates multiple splits per file. Each split
now carries per-row-group statistics extracted during Parquet split
discovery. The optimizer rules merge these per-split statistics
(sum row counts, min-of-mins, max-of-maxes) instead of bailing
out when splits > 1.

Key changes:
- Add SplitRange record to RangeAwareFormatReader SPI
- Add statistics field to FileSplit with boolean-flag serialization
- Add mergeStatistics() to SourceStatisticsSerializer
- ParquetFormatReader extracts per-row-group stats in discoverSplitRanges
- Both PushStatsToExternalSource and PushAggregatesToExternalSource
  merge per-split stats for multi-split queries

title: Disallow TaskType.ANY in ModelConfigurations
body:
A model with a TaskType of ANY is not a valid configuration, since endpoint creation requires that a TaskType is specified. In order to ensure that this behaviour is enforced in tests, which may create models without going through the TaskType validation in
RestPutInferenceModelAction.prepareRequest(), an assert has been added to the constructor for ModelConfigurations.

Many tests were incorrectly using TaskType.ANY, so they have been updated to either use a specific, appropriate task type, or a random task type other than ANY, depending on the test.
author: DonalEvans

reviews.body: I don't see any issues with this PR. LGTM!

title: [9.3] Quantize ST_X, ST_Y and related functions (#140963)
body: Manual backport of https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/140963
author: craigtaverner

commits.message:
Quantize ST_X, ST_Y and related functions (#140963)

Recent [optimizations in geo-grid functions](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/pull/138917) exposed some inconsistencies in other spatial functions that can return results either quantized (doc-values or lucene index) or not (source).

ES|QL typically returns fields from doc-values, for performance reasons. However, for geospatial point data, this means a slight loss of precision, because geo_point and cartesian_point are quantized from two doubles (128bits) down to one long (64bits), in both doc-values and the lucene index (but not stored fields, or source). For all real-world use cases the remaining precision is fine, and something most users are willing to trade for the performance advantages. However, that willingness usually only extends to analytics, and if the user simply returns the original field, they usually want to see the exact original values. For this reason geospatial data is always returned from source in ES|QL, at a huge performance hit. However, we have implemented a number of optimizations that try to make use of doc-values whenever possible, and whenever the user does not return the original points so they will not see the precision loss. As we've expanded the scope of these optimizations, we've encountered a BWC issue with two particular functions, ST_X and ST_Y (both GA), and a less concerning issue with a group of related functions (tech-preview): ST_ENVELOPE, ST_XMAX, ST_XMIN, ST_YMAX and ST_YMIN.

This PR fixes the inconsistency, making ST_X, ST_Y and the envelope functions all produce quantized results, so optimizations do not result in different values.

Users can still get the original values with full prevision simply by not dropping the original geometry field, which will still be read from source. This gives users control over the precision-vs-performance lever. Just drop the original field to maximize performance, or keep it to see the original precision.

commits.message: Quantize ST_X, ST_Y and related functions
Add more information to the docs concerning spatial precision

Refactored all evaluator names to include Cartesian/Geo before WKB/DocValues

This increases similarity and consistency and reduces confusion

Fixed mistake in quantizing geo-envelope using CARTESIAN

Also removed incorrect multiple quantization for doc-values results
Make envelope points accumulator thread-local for memory efficiency
Reduce code duplication using spatial coordinate type in buillder

Fix multi-threading issue with PointVisitor in ST_ENVELOPE et al

We were using a supplier for the results builder, but mistakenly passing in a shared points visitor, instead of making a new one when the builder was initiated.
Reduce the number of evaluators since we've moved GEO/Cartesian into the results builders
Merge branch 'quantize_st_xy' of github.com:craigtaverner/elasticsearch into quantize_st_xy

Disabled tests with inline stats and st_x/st_y on older versions

Since ST_X and ST_Y return slightly different precision in older versions, mixed clusters will return no results due to precision differences in the WHERE clause.
We could consider relaxing equality for spatial types, but that is expensive (needs to convert WKB to Points, quantize and then back to WKB).

title: Unmute flaky csv-spec tests with more logging on warnings context
body:
These tests do not reproduce, even with reported random seeds. We are unmuting them in the hope that the additional logging will bring some clarity to the situations under which they fail.

Of note is that there are 186 tests that have warnings specified, but only 8 issues reported grouped into the following test classes:
* EsqlSpecIT - 1 issue
* MultiClusterSpectIT - 3 issues
* GenerativeForkIT - 4 issues

Five of the eight were related to parsing invalid geospatial types, the other three were change_point, fuse and space-negative.

Six of the eight were reported on main:

Fixes #136249
Fixes #135455
Fixes #134635
Fixes #134254
Fixes #134104
Fixes #133334

Two issues were only muted on 9.2, so we should unmute in the backport: #136285 and #136068
author: craigtaverner

This change unmutes 6 muted tests and adds additional context to several
warning assertions as described.

title: Fix ES|QL docs railroad diagrams comma positions for optional parameters
body:
The SVG images in ES|QL docs had many mistakes with comma positions inside optional parameter groups. 

For example, the bucket command does not include the comma in the optional group, making it look like we need to type a trailing comma:
![bucket](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6a28fb08-32c4-4f23-9d44-451bb3adad9a)

This is fixed to look like this:
![bucket_fixed](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/964dec4d-1b9b-437b-9267-0051d550124e)

Almost twenty images are fixed, while also taking care to handle commas at the end of trailing optional parameters, and complex cases like COALESCE:
![coalesce](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ea2295f6-d19e-4b8d-953b-67a45e39b914)

We also added unit tests for the `RailRoadDiagram` class to make it much easier to test and debug this, as well as reducing the chance that further refinements break anything.
author: craigtaverner

reviews.body: Wonderful!
